data.title: PayPal Undervalued, But Checkout Weakness Limits Upside Amid Earnings Decline - Rating Downgrade
data.description:
Investment Thesis

PayPal Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ: PYPL) stock is down roughly 15% after Q4’25 results, driven by the increasing execution risk regarding the Branded Checkout business line and the unpredictable change of the CEO.

My target is to show what led the company’s board to change the CEO and why’s PYPL business model struggles to convince investors that it is sustainable, despite the marginal revenue and profit expansion over the last quarters.

Branded Checkout: The Main Profit Engine Is Not Performing Effectively

Let me start with the company’s management statements to create the framework of my analysis:

Branded checkout represents over half our profit dollars, and we're confident the product, channel and marketing investments we're making will drive improvement and acceleration over time… Where we haven't made the same progress is in online branded checkout. We've reimagined a product that had been stagnant and underinvested in for years, creating a new value proposition for merchants and consumers, but we were too optimistic about how quickly we could drive change and customer adoption across a massive global user base.

In short, PYPL’s main revenue driver, Branded checkout, doesn’t perform as expected due to the company underestimating how easy it is for adoption by users and merchants. Management accepted the fact that their “execution has not been what it needs to be”, because merchants “require much more hands-on integration support than (they) anticipated”. In addition, macroeconomic conditions and significant competition contribute to the low adoption and poor expansion of the Branded checkout business line.

As a result, active accounts continue to remain relatively flat over the last quarters, while transactions per active account (TPA) show a significant decrease in full year 2025, reflecting the company’s weakness in locking customers onto its platform.

The combination of flat growth in Branded checkout and the higher growth in Enterprise Payments (PSP), Venmo and debit card adoption business lines -which contribute less to profitability on margin base, according to the company- led the transaction take rate to drop from 1.73% in Q4’24 to 1.65% in Q4’25. The transaction take rate is what percentage from every transaction PYPL gains as revenue. However, total revenue increased by 4% YoY, due to the increase in total payment volume (TPV) and due to a more cost-efficient business model.

That said, it’s logic for the transaction margin (TM) -which reflects what the company actually "keeps in its pocket" from revenue – to increase just 3% in Q4’25, mainly driven by Venmo, PSP and credit expansion. TM has a direct impact on the company’s profitability, and as a result operating income margin remains YoY relatively flat in Q4’25.

The company announced some interesting initiatives in order to accelerate the Branded checkout business line, specifically:

We're changing our approach to focus on strategic merchants, representing nearly 25% of our branded checkout volume today but could be much larger… We're now deploying experience and biometrics together as a package more consistently… On presentment, we need competitive placement, including upstream Buy Now, Pay Later messaging and second buttons. And on selection, we must deliver loyalty benefits using rewards and co-marketing agreements with compelling consumer incentives… Our checkout improvements help with conversion, but we can do more to drive repeat usage by combining rewards…our rewards program launching in Europe and in the U.S. in '26, enables consumers to earn and redeem rewards at checkout.

I can summarize the statements above as follows:

  • PYPL focuses on improving users’ experience with biometric and passkey adoption.
  • Actions for its checkout button to be in a better placement, which means better presentment.
  • The company offers motivation for users to select PayPal’s checkout option via rewards and loyalty benefits.
  • All the above initiatives mainly will take place in PYPL’s strategic large merchants and not in all enterprises as was the first plan.

The real question here is whether the above actions can accelerate the company’s most significant business line or not. In my view, the new plan seems to be more strategic with specific goals and actions; however, I can’t see any specific reason why users select PYPL for their transactions instead of any other platform.

PayPal still dominates the global online payments industry, with almost 50% market share in 2025; however, in the US, Apple Pay has a 92% market share because it is ease to use, according to the survey. The company’s actions strategically are in the correct direction but, in my view, PYPL has already delayed acting and supporting its main revenue driver; therefore, it has lost significant ground against the competition, including Google Pay.

An interesting factor is that 88% of Apple Pay users also use PayPal, which means that if PYPL could find a way to convince Apple’s (AAPL) users to choose its payment solutions for various transactions -creating cross-selling flywheel- it could lead to further revenue and profit expansion. Based on the above actions, I don’t believe that PYPL could “steal” Apple users, because PYPL does not offer any different or tech advantaged services. My opinion is, that the ideal payment solution is, on the one hand, easy to use on a daily basis, such as Apple Pay, or on the other hand offers significant benefits. Therefore, I see PYPL’s benefits and loyalty motivation as the only promising growth catalyst; however, the Branded checkout still struggles to expand.

Could The Other Growth Engines Convince the Users and Finally the Investors?

PayPal has various growth catalysts under development except Branded checkout, such as Venmo, Payment Service Provider ((PSP)) solutions - namely Braintree- and credit services like Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL).

Venmo revenue grew 20% YoY, reaching 67M active accounts in Q4’25-and over 100M in FY25- along with 14% in average revenue per active account (ARPA), while TPV increased by 13% marking the fifth consecutive quarter with double-digit growth. According to the company “Venmo is on track to exceed $2 billion in revenue ahead of plan”. Venmo is not just a P2P platform, in contrast, it is a commerce hub on which you pay the merchants, and you have access to credit solutions via BNPL.

Moreover, BNPL delivered over $40B in TPV in 2025 reflecting more than 20% YoY growth, while the appearance of the BNPL option in the payment process increases the Branded checkout volume by 10%. Also, BNPL and its credit solutions contribute the most to the Other Value Added services segment. Under this segment PYPL generates revenue from fees, interest rates and subscription and appears to grow at a higher rate compared to the traditional Transaction segment, giving more diversified revenue streams.

Turning to the PSP business line, volume growth rose to 8% in Q4’25 from 6% in Q3 and 2% in Q1, which reflect strong momentum, while the company improved its profitability within the segment given that it is “roughly doubling (its) net processing yield”. However, at the company level, the mix of PSP, Venmo and debit card adoption growth in combination with flat growth in Branded checkout led to a lower transaction take rate, as mentioned earlier.

Overall, Venmo and PSP business lines contribute about half of PYPL’s 6% transaction margin dollar growth, which means that those two business lines contribute significantly to the company’s total profitability.

Considering all the above, PYPL’s growth engines seem to have strong momentum, given that customers adopt their solutions, except for its core business line, the Branded checkout. The main question here is whether the rest of its growth catalyst could convince investors to value the company with higher multiples as a fintech ecosystem provider and not as payment software company.

In my view, this is not much possible to happen soon. A deep dive into the company’s FY26 guidance shows that despite a share buyback of $6B, which means about 7% shares reduction, the EPS is expected to remain flat or even show a small decline.

In order for EPS to remain flat, given a 7% shares reduction, it means that earnings will drop by at least 7% in FY26, which is an important decrease in profit. In my view, the main reason behind the lower profitability is the company’s high investments mainly towards Branded checkout and BNPL, which will have a negative impact of 3 points to TM dollar growth.

Valuation and Risk Analysis

PE and PEG ratios show a strong undervalued company; however, investors are concerned about the expected weak low single-digit revenue growth for at least the next three years.

Analysts have a target price of about $53, but since 2022 have been continuously decreasing their EPS and revenue guidance, reflecting the company’s poor performance over the last years.

PYPL’s poor performance can be found for several reasons, including competition and execution risk.

First, the company is in transition phase from a payment company towards a commerce platform -as I mentioned in my previous article about PYPL. This transition seems to underperform to expectations, in my view, and requires different skill sets and advanced technology compared to PYPL’s traditional payment-processing business. Even more important is that its traditional business, Branded checkout, shows weak growth trajectory leading to lower profitability, and cannot easily support the company’s higher needs for investments for the rest of its business lines.

Second, high competition has already had a negative impact on the company’s revenue sentiment. If PayPal doesn’t find a way to attract customers again, its leading position in the online payment market, as well as overall in payment industry could significantly shrink in the following years.

A positive sign is its strong FCF -$2.1B in Q4’25 and $6.4B in FY25 - which could fund marketing expenses or growth investments in order for the company to remain competitive.

In conclusion, PayPal appears a strongly undervalued stock with solid financials and is currently undergoing a strategic shift from a payment processor to a commerce platform which seems not to be executed as planned. Its main business line, Branded checkout, seems to have weak performance, while BNPL, PSP and Venmo have significant potential to enhance the company’s revenue base and expand profitability. However, given that these growth engines still cannot fully offset the profit loss due to weak Branded business, investors need to remain cautious about significant revenue and profit expansion after 2–3 years, in my view.

Given all the above, I rate the stock as HOLD, despite its attractive valuation position.

data.title: Visa Is In A Changing Environment With Opportunities And Risks
data.description:
Introduction

Visa (V) has not performed well, declining 11% over the past year. There are several factors that have led the market to question what is otherwise a strong business. These include regulatory and antitrust pressure, as well as a potential fundamental threat to its moat. Real-Time Payment (RTP) systems completely bypass VisaNet and, in some cases, are now more widely used than Visa and Mastercard combined, it is important to explore Visa’s threat and response. Despite these fears, Visa looks as healthy as ever, with growth remaining in the low double digits (apart from net income) and management guiding for low double digit growth for 2026. In this article, I explore Visa’s current strategy and assess the extent of the RTP threat.

Visa’s current strategy

Visa is a network of networks that connect roughly 12 billion cards, bank accounts and digital wallets to 175+ million merchant locations across 200+ countries. Visa acts as a toll collector, earning service revenue which is a percentage of the payment volume, data processing revenue which is a fixed processing fee paid by issuers and acquirers, and cross-border fees for international transactions which offer higher margins.

Other revenue “consists mainly of value-added services (VAS) primarily related to Advisory and Other Services and certain Issuing Solutions”. The increase in other revenues from 3.6% to 7% of gross revenue shows Visa’s pivot towards software-like revenues at zero marginal cost. Visa is becoming less of a “payment toll” and more of a payments infrastructure + SaaS layer. Its VAS, consisting of issuing solutions, acceptance solutions, risk & security solutions and advisory & other services has had incredible growth over the past 3 years, now representing 27% of net revenue.

Visa’s VAS are built on top of its payment network and infrastructure which allows them to scale with minimal CAPEX and very high margins. It already has VisaNet, connections to issuers, acquirers, merchants and the biggest data layer with information about transactions, identities and behavior. The biggest advantage of pivoting towards VAS is that it has minimal incentive fees and requires minimal CAPEX, meaning that its profitability is even higher. I believe that this is a big reason why nearly every profitability metric has increased to its highest levels while CAPEX has remained relatively stable.

Visa’s CEO, Ryan McInerney, has repeatedly outlined this strategy:

“We continue to build and deliver innovations and scalable technologies across the Visa as a Service stack acting as a payments hyperscaler to enable anyone in the ecosystem to build, launch and scale money movement and payments businesses across the globe.”

Management believes VAS TAM to be $520B with some categories being more profitable than others.

New roads that VAS unlocks:

Visa is expanding into multiple service and solution layers including “Visa credentials, agentic commerce, stablecoins as well as B2B and P2P money movement, issuer processing and risk and security.” Visa credentials allow the company to layer multiple services such as tap to pay, Visa Flex Credentials and Tokens. Visa wants to venture into other forms of payment credentials outside of a payment card number (PAN) through the use of tokens, digital IDs and wallet credentials. While Visa has dominated retail payment volume, it has limited presence in P2P, B2B and Bill payments.

Real-Time Payment (RTP) systems have recently emerged as a major technological shift as they allow instantaneous money transfers between bank accounts with very low fees, minimal intermediaries and without using VisaNet. Latin America and Asia are leading the way with Brazil’s PIX, Mexico’s SPEI, India’s UPI, Singapore’s PayNow and many other examples. These RTP systems are run or partially run by central banks and do not use Visa’s network at all. The way I see it is that Visa’s role is largely limited to its VAS layer by offering credentials, fraud protection, APIs, tokenization, etc. This has clearly worked and as more countries adopt RTP systems such as the US’s FedNow, Visa will be the best positioned to offer those services. We are seeing progress in the area with Ryan’s prepared remarks explaining how it is partnering with fintechs around the world to facilitate its capabilities in P2P, B2B and BNPL transactions through Tap to Phone, Visa Flex Credential, a new Cash App Visa Debit Flex Card and Visa Token.

Risks

While some argue RTP cannot replace Visa as it is a network for credit with global acceptance and fraud protection, I believe Visa’s model is fundamentally challenged. RTP is a payment rail where transactions can be funded by debit, credit lines and BNPL loans. It is a simple change to a network with less intermediaries that has already happened in countries like Brazil. Brazil’s largest fintech company, NuBank, already has credit cards that use PIX citing “Pix is ​​growing more and more in the retail and service market, and many merchants offer a discount for using it”. In fact, PIX is the most widely used payment system in Brazil, having processed more transactions than Visa and Mastercard combined. Now 93% of all Brazilian adults use PIX regularly and in 2024 the number of transactions increased 53%. By allowing users to do instant payments using a phone, it has basically removed the use of credit cards with Brazil’s Central Bank Chairman Roberto Campos Neto saying “This system eliminates the need to have a credit card. I think that credit cards will cease to exist at some point soon”. There is the argument that centrally managed companies will never outcompete the private sector due to lack of incentives by the central bank. However, in 2019, 77% of all transactions were made using cash which is a massive cost to the central bank due to a shadow economy. Since its launch in 2020, cash usage in Brazil collapsed to 6%.

With similar RTP systems happening in many other countries and with massive incentives from governments to create these systems, this fundamentally challenges Visa and Mastercard for the first time. In a perfect world where every country has its own RTP system, VisaNet will only be used for international transactions.

Another risk that I see is that despite Visa having one of the best payment networks, Visa is venturing into a sector with fierce competition. Competition is more intense than ever with players in all four of its VAS categories. The same challenges that prevented PayPal (PYPL) from adapting despite its large user base, could also apply to Visa. With Visa venturing into VAS solutions, it will have the highest risks it has had in the past and for me it is impossible to determine the winner in a sector so divided.

Another risk is the litigation and regulatory pressure it has recently received. The DOJ alleges Visa unlawfully maintained monopoly power in “general purpose debit network services” through a mix of de facto exclusivity, punitive pricing and inducements that deterred its customers from using rival debit rails. This risks a constraint on Visa’s U.S. debit network economics. This case is gaining traction and it is a significant antitrust challenge.

Valuation

Visa’s network is being fundamentally challenged and it is currently facing its most consequential U.S. antitrust challenge in decades. With this in mind, its stock is not as cheap as expected, with a P/E of 29 and P/S of 14.7.

I think the market may expect the current litigation challenges to not fundamentally threaten its core business. Its VAS layer is a real opportunity that the market may be excited about as it has great growth and a promising future.

Conclusion

RTP systems fundamentally threaten Visa’s network by completely bypassing it and offering rails with less intermediaries and more benefits. This is already the case in several countries where now more transactions happen using RTP systems than Visa and Mastercard combined. Central banks and governments benefit greatly from RTP systems and I believe that it is inevitable that every major country will have one at some point. While this is a significant threat to its core business worthy of a Sell rating, I do think that Visa’s pivot towards offering its VAS solutions to be the most logical strategic response. With high margins, no incentive costs and minimal CAPEX, I believe this is a low risk high reward move with evidence of successful implementation. Not only this but Visa already has partnerships with many RTP systems that will require a whole set of its VAS solutions. This strategy, combined with the strength of its core business leads to a Buy rating. Given the conflicting signals between risk and opportunity and because I would like to see more evidence regarding its VAS and RTP partnerships, I am recommending a Hold.

data.title: Buy Value-Oriented Covered Call ETFs For Relaxed Passive Income
data.description:
Setting the scene

Today's markets are anything but relaxing.

Most equities (SPY) are down. Bonds (BND) and the so called "60/40" approach (AOR) have once again disappointed certain investors.

The recent sell-off that has been driven by soaring oil prices and their knock-on effects to inflation, interest rates and overall economic health have negatively affected even safest areas of market in which, say, durable income investors tend to seek shelter.

For example, REITs (VNQ), high-quality dividend growth (SCHD) and infrastructure (UTF) also have trended downwards since the war in Iran began.

I think that two things are pretty clear (or at least embody very high odds of happening):

1. Growth prospects for many high multiple names (but in reality almost across the board) have weakened.

Just think of soaring oil prices introducing headwinds to consumer spending, AI CapEx becoming increasingly more expensive, supply chain disruptions (e.g. helium) and less accommodative financing costs.

It is hard to imagine how against this kind of backdrop the companies could justify their sky-high valuation multiples.

2. Volatility is likely to remain higher for longer.

The combination of rich valuations, very tight spreads and mounting risks in the system is the perfect recipe for a heightened volatility.

In the chart below we can see how VIX which measures the implied volatility for the S&P 500 has doubled since the start of the year:

The same could be said about almost any other volatility measurement index out there, including the MOVE index (volatility of U.S. Treasury yields) that has surged higher this year.

The "Almost Perfect" Setup for Covered Calls

It is clear that high-growth and richly priced equities are probably not the best picks out there amidst the various economic headwinds and increased uncertainty.

Currently, it seems that we have entered into a risk-off phase that can last for quite some time, like until valuations revert back to historical norms (averages), until the war in Iran is comes to an end (who know how long it could).

All this creates a strong case for bond-like exposures where the securities do not have to justify their valuation multiples but instead deliver the promised coupons and principals. As I will describe it below, the value-tilted covered call ETF exposures introduce a similar pay-off profile to a high-yield bond: i.e., attractive coupon (think of collected option premiums), limited price appreciation potential (due to the written options capping upside) and protected downside (stable principal).

At the same time, there is of course the duration risks which stems from higher for longer interest rate environment, or even worse inching up long-term yields.

As I briefly commented above, this is the reason why bonds are down and the "60/40 portfolio strategy" does not deliver.

Now, this is where covered call ETFs could come in and bridge the gap.

At their core, covered call ETFs act like high-yield bonds. Think of it:

  • The upside potential is limited by the written option strike value. Namely, the payoff profile is capped just like for high-yield bonds - i.e., enticing coupons and some price appreciation of the underlying principal if the security was bought at a discount to par.
  • The downside is fully open until the underlying reaches zero adjusted for collected option premiums (similar to high-yield bonds).

In other words, by venturing into covered call ETFs we are inherently assuming less equity-like risk (and return) and more bond-like risk (and return).

However, there still are some differences that are largely driven by the choice of the underlying index or the long position against which covered call ETFs sell option contracts.

For example, we have to remember that the downside for most covered call ETF instruments remains almost identical to that of the underlying index against which the calls are sold. So, it won't help shied our portfolios from underperforming growth names if we bias our picks towards the S&P 500 or the Nasdaq-100 based covered call ETFs. Even though we might still be better off by riding the growth-name sell-off with growth covered call ETF products (because of the collected option premiums), the NAV decay will still be there leading to potentially lower and lower income streams (i.e., falling income and falling NAV; not what we could considered a strong hedge).

Similarly, unless we go with the NEOS Enhanced Income Aggregate Bond ETF (BNDI) which has selected fixed income as its underlying, we can nicely avoid the duration risk or the risk of losing money from interest rates going up (especially the long-term yields).

In essence, by cherry picking the right covered call ETF products we can:

  • Deemphasize growth factors and assume more predictable bond-like factors.
  • Mitigate duration risk that would be a major issue for fixed income bets.
  • Profit from the elevated uncertainty (or volatility).
  • And my favorite: hedge our portfolios in a manner that boosts our current income stream.

How to Play It

The are two broad choices we have to make:

  1. Strategy selection.
  2. Security selection.

When it comes to strategy selection it boils down to the following plays:

  • At-the-money (or ATM) covered calls. Caps upside potential to the price level that is very close to the current market price and receives in return richer option premiums.
  • Zero-day expiry (or 0DTE) covered calls. Even more aggressive option strategy than the ATM one. Covered call options are sold on a daily basis on usually very tight out-of-moneyness levels. For example, there could be a huge opportunity cost that is associated with the underlying experiencing a huge drawdown in a single day and then bouncing back the next day (where 0DTEs would have very limited participation).
  • Out-of-the-money (or OTM) covered calls. This is my favorite. It strikes a balance between option income harvesting and leaving some upside potential in the system. Typically, OTM covered call ETF products write their calls 10% to 20% above the current market price of the underlying with a option contract duration of ~30 days.

In the chart below I have highlighted how these consequences of various upside potential caps can manifest themselves in an environment (tariff tantrum) in which the index has many ups and downs (e.g., dips followed by recovery processes):

The NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), which is a pure-play OTM fund has delivered the closest return statistic to the index. The Global X Covered Call ETF (QYLD) due to ATM options has failed to fully participate in the recovery. For the Roundhill Innov-100 0DTE Covered Call Strat ETF (QDTE) the story is even worse.

While I would not suggest leaning into any of these instruments if the expectation is that the high valuation growth names might be subject to further drawdowns, if I had to choose one of them I would go with QQQI.

The reason is simple: it is an OTM covered call which in the case of potential recovery (bullish sentiment coming back) would translate to the smallest opportunity cost.

So, this is what I'm talking about.

Given the historical patterns of the market where after periodic dips new record highs have followed and that the Fed could potentially step in with interest rate cuts to stabilize the labor market (or even go big on the balance sheet expansion), I see a notable merit of leaving some upside potential open.

Now, once we have established that OTM covered calls might be optimal instruments to consider, the next (and final) question in line is how to go about individual security selection.

There is a huge variety in the OTM covered call space which is very good.

It means that we can be more nuanced and lean into specific factors that would suit better in this particular environment.

I have three points to make here.

First, given what I outlined above my preference would be to stay away from richly priced high-growth names. In my view, this is the area which is susceptible to another leg-down. So, if there is a drawdown in, say, the Nasdaq-100, the OTM covered call ETFs that sell their options against this index will very likely fall accordingly.

Here is an example how it works in practice: based on QQQI, the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) and the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) (one of the largest QQQ-linked OTM covered calls):

I would rather focus on OTM covered call ETFs that can enjoy the benefit of elevated volatility and shield their downside from, say, growth-focused equity valuation repricing.

Second, these ETFs are not based on traditional fixed income products. While they do indeed have better downside protection than QQQ-tied equity constituents, there are two new issues: (1) duration risk kicks in (as described above) and (2) the yields are not that enticing.

BNDI's price is down by ~2% and the current yield stands only at 5.8% (too low for covered call ETF product, in my view).

The NEOS Enhanced Income 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (CSHI) has a similar issue although it is tilted toward short-term interest rate segment in which duration risk is not as material. CSHI has remained largely flat this year. It offers only ~4.9% in yield.

Third, this is where the rubber meets the road.

In my view, OTM covered call ETFs which are exposed to high-quality or value equity underlying positions are the most promising picks to secure downside protections, while clipping increasingly more enticing "coupons".

The idea is to get rid of the high-growth equity risks, but not gravitate too far away from the equity-like risk and return factors. The latter is critical to capture tangible current income streams and really profit from heightened volatility. Also, by sticking to value exposures we automatically mitigate the risk of experiencing negative alpha from a sudden and strong recovery that might take place in QQQ/SPY indices.

The NEOS MLP & Energy Infrastructure High Income ETF (MLPI) and the NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) are two solid examples here.

They both are dynamically managed OTM covered call ETFs that are based on value-tilted underlying indices. MLPI invests sell OTM covered calls against midstream companies, the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and the NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) do that against REITs (VNQ).

The yields (I've annualized them based on the most recent month distributions) for MLPI and IYRI are 14.2% and 11.4%, accordingly. They both have a management fee of 0.68% and a "physical holdings" approach, meaning that they actually do own the underlying assets - i.e., they are not synthetically created through complex option positions.

Other than that, the secret sauce lies stems from their underlying holdings:

  • Midstream and real estate are inherently value plays that derive their cash flows from long-term contracts (it reduces the cyclicality).
  • Their valuations are not sky-high which means that they do not have to justify them in an environment which is more and more challenging.
  • These are real assets which tend to perform well in inflationary environments.
  • Their core yields are higher than for, say, the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq-100 that contribute nicely to MLPI and IYRI total dividend yields.

I encourage you to explore my ticker-specific articles on MLPI and IYRI by clicking here and here, respectively.

Key Risks

The key risk that is associated with OTM covered call ETF is that even though the OTM factor helps, we've to still be ready to experience negative alpha (or the opportunity cost of not capturing full upside of the underlying index).

While MLPI and IYRI stand a good chance of beating potentially falling "QQQ names", they might underperform pure-play midstream and REITs investments, respectively.

However, in case of sidelines trading or gradually decreasing indices, MLPI and IYRI could generate alpha. If the volatility levels remain this high going forward (or frequently jump up and down), the odds for MLPI and IYRI would certainly go up.

And the key risk that I see for MLPI is suddenly falling oil prices that might stem from the resolved conflict in Iran. Soaring oil prices have pumped up the midstream valuations and thus are likely to do the same thing in the opposite direction (if prices fall). Yet, I doubt that the war will be over any time soon and I even more doubt that it will be a smooth sailing back to the pre-war oil prices once the fighting is over.

For IYRI the key risk, in my view, is the spiking long-term yields. If the defects continue to deepen and the war drags for a very long time thereby resulting in higher for longer oil prices, the long-term inflation expectations could inch up. In such a case, the long-term yield could follow accordingly which would yet again penalize REITs. However, as an offset to this I see the Fed stepping in to keep the long-term yield segment balanced (i.e., buying up long-term bonds in order to stabilize the yields). Plus, we have to remember that while REITs are sensitive to long-term yields the consequences will be nowhere near those of the traditional fixed income securities (i.e., REITs are hybrid instruments).

Final Remarks

In a nutshell, it is a great time to buy value-oriented OTM covered call ETFs to shield portfolios from potential sell-off risks in a high-income manner.

The focus on value index (exposure) based covered call ETFs should allow investors to avoid headwinds from valuation compression across the richly priced growth areas of the market. Namely, the value play which usually comes in vogue when the risk levels in the system rise should be beneficial to NAV protection and thus covered call ETF income stability.

Of course, there might be some bumps in the value factor as well but it is unlikely that they will be as severe as for growth names. And given that the volatility remains elevated, the richer option premium income could also offset some of the NAV drag.

Finally, I see this specific strategy working as long as the VIX remains elevated which is above 15 - 17x (currently, it is ~30x). Falling back to low volatility environment would most likely imply that overall uncertainty in the system has faded and that the growth is likely to come back with a vengeance. However, the benefit of implementing the value-oriented OTM covered call ETF approach is that the out-of-moneyness factor should leave us with an opportunity to participate in a system-wide recovery (equity rally).

data.title: Sysco - Acquisition Of Jetro Comes With Some Questions
data.description:
Shares of Sysco (SYY) plunged following the news that the company announced a massive deal to acquire Jetro Restaurant Depot in a deal valued at nearly $30 billion.

Investors fear the change in strategic direction, as Sysco is buying an attractive and higher-margin peer operating in a decent market, yet the issue is that the company will see a massive increase in leverage.

Some strategic questions, leverage incurred, and general jitteriness about the large deal created a massive sell-off in the share price. While that looks appealing in isolation, I am hard-pressed to buy the dip today but am keen on getting involved in further setbacks here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A action, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

A Large Deal

Sysco has reached a deal to acquire premier Cash & Carry food wholesaler Jetro Restaurant Depot in what the company calls a transformative deal. The company touts the strategic rationale of entering a high-margin, growing, and resilient Cash & Carry channel.

Deal terms call for a $21.6 billion cash component and 91.5 million shares of Sysco to be issued to investors in Jetro. Valued at $81.80 per share ahead of the news, this translates into a $29.1 billion deal, valuing the business at 14.6 times operating income, seen around $2.0 billion each year.

The company runs 166 large-format warehouse stores across 35 states to serve over 700,000 independent restaurants and foodservice operators with fresh and low-priced goods, with clients seeking delivery and white glove service. These are generally lucrative operations, with $2.1 billion in EBITDA reported on $16 billion in sales, valuing the operations at 1.8 times sales, these carrying healthy 13% EBITDA margins.

The company sees about $250 million in synergies in three years post-closing, equal to about 12.5% of operating income acquired, driven by procurement gains and supply chain optimization. This modeling excludes revenue synergies. Even with just part of the synergies seen realised in year one post-closing, mid-to high-single-digit earnings per share accretion is seen in the first year.

A Big Deal

The deal adds significantly to Sysco. Based on the 2025 results, that is, for the fiscal year that ended last summer, Sysco reports annual revenues of $81.4 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $4.3 billion with adjusted earnings of $2.2 billion, working down to a $4.46 per share number.

Trading at $80, the 483 million shares outstanding were valued at $38.6 billion, or $50.8 billion if net debt is included. That valued the operations at 0.6 times sales, about 12 times EBITDA, with shares trading around 18 times earnings.

In that light, a 1.8 times sales multiple for Jetro marks a giant premium, but this comes as EBITDA margins of 13% are superior to Sysco's margins just north of 5%. Pro forma revenues are seen around $100 billion, with EBITDA is seen at $6.4 billion, so I can see why this is accretive. Sysco operates with a depreciation intensity of just over 1% of sales, and if we apply a 2% mark for Jetro, its EBIT contribution is seen around $1.8 billion.

Foregone interest income, but moreover interest expenses incurred in over $21 billion in additional debt, will easily result in additional interest expenses of $1.0-$1.3 billion, but that still would leave an earnings contribution of $500 million ahead of dilution and taxes.

With investors in Jetro given a 16% equity stake in the pro forma share count, I see the total share count increase by 19% to 575 million shares. The approximately 92 million issued shares, and existing shares outstanding, will see a pre-tax earnings contribution of around $500 million, or about $400 million after taxes, which makes that modest earnings per share growth is seen.

However, every dollar in deleveraging, but moreover synergies, are highly accretive, with synergies over time having the potential to boost pre-tax earnings by $0.43 per share.

Needless to say, net debt will jump to about $33 billion here, and while EBITDA is seen at $6.4 billion, that results in sky-high leverage of around 5 times. This happens just at a time when the economy and even consumer spending in restaurants might be shaky given the turmoil out there. Sysco is quick to point out that leverage is seen around 4.5 times upon closing, seen in the third quarter of its fiscal year 2027, giving the company quite some time to deleverage ahead of closing.

The Market Is Not Taking It

While Jetro is awarded premium sale multiples, overall EBITDA multiples look fair as investors likely fear the strategic rationale and leverage overhang. Shares are down $12, or 15%, in response to the deal announcement. Including the to-be-issued shares, that is about $7 billion in value going up in smoke, a quarter of the applied deal tag to Jetro.

The company reiterated its 2026 guidance for earnings per share to come in at the high-end of the $4.50-$4.60 per share guidance. Factoring in some mid-single-digit earnings per share accretion from the deal, forward earnings are seen between roughly $4.75-$5.00 per share. This meant that shares traded at 17-18 times earnings heading into the news report, as this multiple has fallen to 14-15 times following the accretion, but mostly the setback is at a higher price.

What Now?

The reality is that I like the strategic rationale, although the counterargument could be made that the deal is causing a distraction from its focus here as well. Applied valuation multiples are very reasonable, certainly factoring in the setback in the share price of Sysco, driven by a massive increase in leverage, and somewhat modest near-term earnings per share accretion.

Amidst all this, I was never a massive fan of Sysco from the beginning. The company is a long-term value creator and consolidator, and subsequently was awarded premium valuation multiples in the past. Shares have been trading in a $70-$90 range since the outset of the pandemic, which, combined with growing earnings, made that valuation multiples came in over time.

Having previously risen to a high of $90 after the business saw some momentum after a period of sluggishness, shares have seen quite a setback here, although the reality is that shares traded at current levels as recently as early January!

The setback in isolation looks compelling, yet the reality is that I fear the overhang and potential strategic drawbacks, which make it so that I am not automatically buying the dip yet. A 14-15 times multiple looks compelling, yet leverage is very high, and the overall outlook is rather uncertain here.

Given all this, I am awaiting a period of consolidation and perhaps further pullback in relation to the deal. If shares are to fall to levels in the lower $60s, I see enough of a risk-reward here, to get involved here, given the leverage added here.

data.title: Instacart: The Growth Stalled For Now
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been over a year since the last time I covered Maplebear Inc. (CART), or Instacart, so I thought it would be a good idea to see how it performed and what is still ahead for it. Since the first article, the company’s share price has declined by over 18% while the SPY (SPY) was up 5%, and that is after the brutal macroeconomic week or so.

I can see potential in CART, but I need proof that it can grow yet again.

Briefly on Performance

Starting from the top, CART’s revenues grow at a respectable pace of around 10% on average for the last year. Nothing mind-blowing. Looking at the latest figures from its 10-K for 2025, revenues came in at around $3.75B, up 11%. GTV, or gross transaction value, came in at over $37.2B, up 11% y/y, while orders came in at 338.8m, an increase of 15% y/y. GTV growth can be mainly attributed to the increase in orders this past year. GTV was also partly offset by the decrease in average order value.

Going over to the company’s margin development in the last year, we can see that gross margins decreased slightly from 75% to 74% in 2025. There wasn’t anything special that made the margins decrease by 100bps, except for the fact that the cost of revenues grew faster than revenue. These are the company’s words. Cost of revenues grew 18%. On an adjusted basis, however, we can see that adjusted EBITDA increase 23% to $1.087B, giving us an adjusted EBITDA margin of 29%, up 300bps from the previous year.

On the back of the decent performance, the company’s other key metrics also improved and are quite attractive. CART’s ROA and ROE are at or close to where I’d like to see them, while the company’s ROTC is standing firm at over 10%, which tells me there is a decent moat and pricing power.

Going over the company’s financial position, CART finished the year with close to $1.3B in cash and equivalents, against zero long-term debt, and very little operating lease liabilities. This is a great position for a company that recently IPO’ed and already turned profitable. All that cash that is generated, which was $971m for 2025 (up 41% y/y), can be used to further the growth of the company and continue to gain market share from competitors and innovate.

This financial position can also be used to reward shareholders through share buybacks or dividends, but I prefer the former. At the end of the year, the company still had around $671m available for repurchase under the agreement, and in November of last year, the company entered into an accelerated share repurchase agreement for $250m. I would say the company retired 6m-7m in shares at the end of January 2026 once the ASR was completed. In the whole year, the company repurchased around $1.4B worth of shares, and $1.1B worth of shares in Q4 alone.

Overall, we can see that the aggressive cost-cutting of the last two years has paid off quite a bit. The company quickly turned its unprofitable business into one that is generating quite good earnings already. The jump in profitability wasn’t as impressive as we saw last year, but there was still an improvement. The only thing that I can find that could be worrisome is the fact that there wasn’t much change in profitability after one more year. Since the company slowed down there, the share price came down, I would say.

Comments on the Outlook

Guidance

The management didn’t provide concrete numbers for revenues for Q1 ’26, but did guide Q1 GTV to be in the range of $10.25B- $01.275, which is a growth of 11%-13% y/y. Adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $280m-$290m. while advertising and other revenues are expected to grow 11%-14%. The management also said they are expecting adjusted EBITDA growth to outpace GTV growth, with this rate to moderate.

Analysts are expecting the company to make around 92 cents and 57 cents of adjusted and GAAP EPS on around $1.01B in revenues. It is also important to note that there have been 16 upward revisions and 1 downward revision in the last 90 days.

New NYC ruling

This is one of the more recent news items regarding CART and how it is going to affect its operations going forward. The NYC law that will go into effect in April requires all delivery drivers to get paid a minimum hourly wage of $21.44 per hour, excluding tips. And that is not for the time you drive and deliver, but rather for the time logged on to the respective platforms. Of course, that doesn’t mean the driver will still be paid on active time on the app, so they cannot collect the minimum wage by logging in. At least in that sense, it is fair, but the companies still don’t like that because the new rule still means that they must pay more for a period when workers are available but not constantly delivering. Their cost base rises. This means CART’s margins may suffer in this region.

What CART and all other platforms in NYC have done to somewhat offset the increase is introduce a surcharge, which is passed on to the consumers. $5.99 in the case of CART. So, on top of all other fees that customers already pay, they will now see another $5.99 charge specifically for NYC. And at first, I thought this was bad for CART. I thought that they would alienate their customers and make them switch to other platforms, but that was not correct. Every delivery app in NYC has some sort of surcharge in place right now because of this new law, so of course, the company is going to do the same. Everyone’s doing it, why not them? This will help its margins, just not too sure how much, and whether that would be enough to maintain its strong software margins, or the new law will make the region unprofitable?

The latest numbers for CART that I found regarding how big New York State is were back in Q2 ’24. Back then, the entire state accounted for 15% of total revenues. I’m sure NYC will be the largest contributor in the state overall, but not the whole 15%. It’s material, but I don’t think this law is going to put the whole company in jeopardy. Regarding alienating customers in NYC, I think many are wealthy enough to absorb the surcharge. It may be a little greedy of these companies to apply the fee to customers. It’s not very friendly at all, and they could easily afford to absorb it themselves, but I also think customers in NYC probably won’t even notice the increase. The median income in NYC in 2024 was almost $86K, which is most likely higher in 2025, but we don’t have that information just yet.

The Drivers

Let’s see what the company has in store for us in terms of growth. The management expects to further monetize advertising and data, which are seen as high-margin growth engines going forward. Carrot Ads is expanding to more than 310 retailer-owned sites and well over 9,000 brands that advertise on the platform. Advertising and other revenue grew to become a $1B segment for CART and has sustained that 11% annual growth. Honestly, I would like to see this growing at a much faster pace because even when I covered the company last January, Carrot Ads grew at the same rate, but the management said to expect “unmatched sales growth,” and 11% is not it, in my opinion. I will always be a proponent of Ads. It’s a very attractive business if you have the customers and data, which I think CART does; however, I would like to see more innovation to grow the segment, as it does have a lot of potential to impress.

Cannot have outlook comments without mentioning some sort of AI involvement, and here, CART is experimenting with it through product efficiency. The management said AI has increased engineering output by around 40%, and even in the most recent conference, the CEO, Chris Rogers, said that some of the engineers saw their output doubling, to 80% higher. On top of this, CART is working on the next big hype, the agentic AI that will assist shoppers, which is kind of interesting, and I am curious to see how it is going to pan out.

Valuation

Let’s do a quick valuation model to give us a better idea of where the company stands in terms of its fair value. Below are the assumptions I used.

Analysts expect 10% growth in sales next year that will taper off, so that is what I essentially did. For margins, I used adjusted numbers, as that is what is widely used for most of the companies. The rest I kept close to the company’s averages. Additionally, I used 10% as my discount and 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. With these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And these projections give us the following fair value.

Investor Takeaway

I can only speak for myself, and for me, CART is still not quite there. There isn’t enough room for growth, and I am worried about how this new law is going to affect the company’s operations in general. I agree with a Hold rating. There is no need to sell, so hold on to your shares and see where the company goes over the next while. A bottom could be near, unless something fundamentally is going to change. I can see potential in the future, but I need to see what happens to its margins and overall profitability over the next few quarters. There was no growth this past year, and that is a little worrying.

data.title: Newmont: Top Gold Miner Trading At 25% Discount
data.description:
Newmont Corporation Update

I rated Newmont (NEM) a Buy in May 2023 with an article called Newmont Mining: A Gold Mine Of Opportunities Ahead, when the stock was around $48. It’s now trading near $102, up 109%.

So why revisit the story now? Because Newmont just pulled back 25% from its January highs near $135. Gold has corrected from above $5,500 to around $4,430. The Iran conflict and rate uncertainty, and a broader risk-off move in commodities have pushed this stock down to levels where the valuation is starting to look interesting again. This isn't cheap on an absolute basis (Newmont never really is). But I think the setup favors buyers here.

Last year Newmont put up $22.7 billion in revenue, $7.3 billion in free cash flow, and produced 5.9 million gold ounces at a by-product AISC of $1,358/oz. Every one of those numbers was a record. And the balance sheet looks better than it ever has, with the company paying down $3.4 billion in debt, returning $3+ billion to shareholders (via dividends and buybacks), and yet still ending the year with $2.1 billion in net cash.

On the downside, you probably won’t triple your money from here, so if you're seeking higher leverage to gold prices and are willing to take on more risk, look elsewhere. But on a risk-adjusted basis, Newmont at $102 is a solid buy, and under $100 I’d be looking to add aggressively.

2025 Was A Record Year Across The Board

Let's run through Newmont's Q4 and full-year 2025 results. Newmont posted $7.6 billion in adjusted net income last year. $6.89 per diluted share. Free cash flow was $7.3 billion for 2025, and $2.8 billion of that came in the fourth quarter (or $11.2 billion annualized).

Production finished at 5.9 million attributable gold ounces, plus 28 million ounces of silver and 135,000 tonnes of copper.

By-product AISC came in at $1,358 per ounce, which at last year's average realized gold prices translated to margins of roughly $2,800 per ounce. At today's gold price near $4,430, you're looking at over $3,000 in margin on every ounce Newmont pulls out of the ground. Most peers can't touch that, and it's the by-product credits that make the difference.

2026 Guidance

Things are looking good for 2026; guidance calls for approximately 5.3 million gold ounces at a by-product AISC of $1,680/oz. Costs are stepping up due to royalty inflation and sustaining capital investment, but at $4,400+ gold, the implied margin is still north of $2,700 per ounce.

Balance Sheet And Capital Return

This is the part that doesn't get enough attention. Newmont reduced debt by $3.4 billion in 2025 and ended the year in a net cash position of $2+ billion.

Cash on hand was $7.6 billion, with total liquidity at $11.6 billion. For a gold miner, in a sector where just 10 years ago senior gold miners carried huge net debt positions, that's extraordinary. In 2014, for example, Newmont's net debt crept over $5 billion.

The capital return story is equally strong. Newmont returned $3.4 billion to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks last year.

There's still $2.4 billion left on the $6 billion buyback authorization, and management has been clear they want to put excess cash toward repurchases on a ratable basis going forward. I like that. At a forward P/E near 10.8x, buying back your own stock is one of the highest-return uses of capital available to them. Every share retired at these levels makes the per-share economics better for everyone who stays.

Newmont's Updated Valuation

Metric

Value

Stock Price (Mar. 27, 2026)

$102

Market Cap

$111 billion

Shares Outstanding

1.09 billion

Forward P/E (2026E)

10.8

Forward EV/EBITDA

5.7

Dividend Yield

~1.0%

Cash Position

$7.6 billion

Net Cash

$2.1 billion

Source: Seeking Alpha, NEM Valuation Grade

A forward P/E of 10.8x for the world's largest gold producer is not expensive. It's not screaming-cheap either, and I want to be honest about that.

Newmont rarely trades at single-digit multiples the way B2Gold (BTG) or Kinross (KGC) can. You're paying a premium for asset quality, jurisdictional diversity, and scale.

But relative to its own five-year average forward P/E of roughly 18x, the current multiple represents a meaningful discount. The stock isn't nearly as cheap as it was in early 2025, but this is a much stronger profitable company in the present day.

And relative to the $4,400 gold environment, which should drive consensus EPS north of $9 for 2026, the stock looks reasonably priced.

Reserves, Jurisdiction, And Downside Protection

Newmont has 118 million ounces of gold reserves and 149 million ounces in resources, and can produce gold for decades based on these ounces. More importantly, the geographic mix is heavily weighted toward stable jurisdictions. Roughly 30% of gold reserves sit in North America. Another 25% in Australia. South America accounts for 20%, Papua New Guinea 18%, and Ghana just 7%.

If you're worried about jurisdictional risk in gold mining (it should be something you at least bake into your analysis), Newmont's portfolio is about as good as it gets outside of pure-play North American names.

Another positive: Newmont holds 13 million tonnes of copper reserves and 19 million tonnes in resources, predominantly from Cadia in Australia and the Red Chris project in British Columbia. Copper trades at $5.48/lb and is up 9% over the past year.

AI-driven data center demand is pushing copper into a structural deficit, and so this is optionality that most gold investors aren't pricing. It also provides real downside protection. If gold corrects sharply, the copper and silver by-product credits keep Newmont's cost structure competitive in a way that pure gold producers can't match.

Growth Pipeline: Three Projects Worth Watching

Newmont has three major development projects that should add meaningful production through the end of the decade.

  • The one I keep coming back to is Cadia Panel Caves in Australia. Over 5 million ounces of gold reserves and 1 million tonnes of copper sitting behind a ~$370 million annual spend, with cave completion targeted for 2029. That's probably the single most valuable development project in the entire gold sector right now, and it doesn't get talked about nearly enough.
  • Tanami Expansion 2 is further along, with $1.3 billion of the $1.7-1.8 billion total already spent and a 1.5km production shaft on track for H2 2027 completion.
  • And then there's Lihir Nearshore Barrier in PNG, which just got full funds approval in February. The mine life extends beyond 2040 and provides over 5 million ounces of gold production.
  • Combined, these three projects represent north of $800 million in 2026 capital spend, which is obviously a lot of capex. But the payoff, if Newmont executes, is a production profile that stays at or above current levels for the next 15 years.

Newmont also recently committed to investing $800 million in its Cerro Negro operation in Argentina, signaling confidence in the improving investment climate under the Milei administration. That's a meaningful vote of confidence in a jurisdiction that hasn't always been kind to miners.

The Risks Are Real

I wouldn't be doing readers a service here if I didn't point out some of the key risks of investing in Newmont Mining, so here are the biggest ones to consider.

Gold price sensitivity. This is the big one. Gold has already corrected more than 20% from its January highs. If the Iran situation de-escalates further or the Fed surprises with a more hawkish stance, gold could test the $4,000 level. At $4,000 gold and $1,680 AISC, Newmont's margin per ounce drops to $2,320, which is still very healthy but would compress earnings meaningfully from current consensus.

You can see below the impact gold prices has had on Newmont's gross profit margins, which briefly went negative in January 2024 with gold trading 50% lower than the current spot price.

Rising costs. The 2026 AISC guidance of $1,680/oz is a step up from FY2025's $1,358. Management attributes this to higher royalty payments at elevated gold prices, increased sustaining capital, and the absence of some non-core assets that were lower-cost producers. This is real cost inflation, not just rounding. Investors should watch whether the trend continues.

Execution risk on three major projects simultaneously. Tanami, Cadia, and Lihir collectively represent over $800 million in 2026 capital spend. Mining project timelines slip. Budgets overrun. Newmont's track record here is decent but not perfect.

Ghana and PNG exposure. Newmont's portfolio is predominantly Tier 1, in the most favorable mining jurisdictions in the world. But investors should note that the Ahafo mine in Ghana and the Lihir operation in Papua New Guinea carry jurisdictional risks. Regulatory changes, tax increases, or political instability in either country could impact operations.

Bottom Line: Buy

Newmont is not the cheapest gold miner I cover. If you want deep value, look at B2Gold at 6x forward earnings or the combined Coeur-New Gold (CDE) entity. If you want the cheapest silver leverage, Silvercorp Metals (SVM) at 6x is hard to beat.

But Newmont gives you something those names can't: the largest reserve base in the world, best-in-class jurisdictional diversity, a fortress balance sheet with $2.1 billion in net cash, and the management discipline to return $3.4 billion to shareholders in a single year. You won't triple your money. That's not what this stock is for. What it does is give you high-quality, diversified gold exposure with meaningful downside protection from copper and silver by-products.

I think this stock is likely to outperform peers, as measured by the VanEck gold miners index (GDX), going forward. At $102, with gold at $4,430 and the forward P/E sitting at 10.8x, the risk-adjusted setup is attractive. Under $100, I'd be adding more aggressively.

data.title: Gevo Has A Big Opportunity, But The Shares Look Fully Valued
data.description:
Thesis

If I had to sum it up, Gevo, Inc. (GEVO) is trying to become a green version of an oil company. They turn corn and other plant material into fuels that work almost exactly like gasoline, diesel, or jet fuel, but with significantly lower carbon emissions. At least for me, the really interesting part is that airlines, trucks, and cars can use Gevo’s fuels without modifying their engines or infrastructure.

You can think of the way they make money in a few buckets. First, there's selling sustainable fuel, especially jet fuel. Their biggest play is something called SAF (sustainable aviation fuel). They also produce and sell RND (renewable natural gas), license their technology, and generate revenue from carbon credits and incentives.

In terms of performance, this year, particularly post-earnings, GEVO’s been on a tear. And just yesterday, shares climbed another 10%. But taking everything into account, after reviewing the latest data and fundamentals, I’m neutral. The value proposition works on paper, but with so much hinging on execution, the risk-reward doesn’t tilt much in investors’ favor.

Gevo: Transition to Multi-Revenue Clean Energy Company

I think the stock going up yesterday makes sense overall. But to me, the more important takeaway from Gevo’s second ethanol plant in North Dakota is that management seems to believe the Richardton project can be copied and repeated in other locations. In other words, they’re betting this whole stack of ethanol, carbon capture, sequestration, and eventually alcohol-to-jet actually works better as a package.

  • Ethanol production (2025): 69M gallons
  • Carbon captured (2025): 173,000 metric tons
  • Carbon credit inventory (Q4 2025): ~30,000 tons.

Before, I think investors viewed Gevo as a company focused on producing SAF in the future, which would require a lot of time and money to get there. But now, the story is changing. It’s starting to look more like a business that can already make money from carbon-related opportunities today. Gevo’s own announcement also made it clear that they believe this project is less risky than starting from scratch somewhere else. That’s because they can use existing carbon storage infrastructure, available underground space to store CO2, and engineering work that’s already been done.

  • Voluntary carbon credit pricing: $100–$300/ton
  • LCFS carbon pricing range: ~$50–$250+/ton.

The quality of its revenue has improved because the company isn’t depending only on the long-term goal of producing SAF anymore. Now, Gevo has a working ethanol facility that produces fuel, animal feed, oil, and captured carbon. Management also emphasized multiple times that carbon is being treated as a main product, not just an extra bonus. That’s important because the biggest positive takeaway from the quarter is they now have more ways to make money and adjust their business depending on what works best.

  • Q4 2025 Adjusted EBITDA: ~$8M
  • Q4 2025 Operating cash flow: $20M
  • FY 2025 Adjusted EBITDA: $16M
  • Adjusted EBITDA target (near-term): $40M annually (~$10M/quarter).

So if low-carbon fuel prices are strong, they can just keep the carbon value bundled into those fuel sales. But if carbon credit markets are paying more, they can split it out and sell carbon removal credits instead. Management calls that flexibility a real competitive advantage. That seems reasonable. However, it also makes the business harder to read, with the revenue mix moving around from quarter to quarter. And that could make their results harder to compare over time than those of traditional ethanol producers, which have a simpler, more consistent business model.

  • Production tax credits sold (2025): $52M
  • Cash received from tax credits (2025): $41M
  • Expected 45Z credit value (2026): ~$0.90/gallon
  • Incremental benefit from CI improvement: +$0.10/gallon
  • CI score reduction: 6–7 points.

Margins and cash flow looked better too. It seems like that’s coming from operations, not just promotion. The plant ran reliably. Yields were strong. Sequestration beat their own targets. Management’s main point was that things like tax credits, selling low-carbon fuel, and starting to earn money from carbon credits are helping reduce the financial strain that usually comes with a clean fuels company that’s still in its early growth stage. I don’t want to make the progress sound bigger than it is. Keep in mind, Gevo remains dependent on ethanol pricing, fluctuations in environmental credit markets, and decisions around the timing of carbon credit sales.

Meanwhile, government policies outside the company are now helping Gevo more than they were just a few weeks ago. The EPA has officially set strong Renewable Fuel Standard targets for the next two years while keeping the requirement for conventional ethanol at the same level. In yesterday’s announcement, Gevo anchored its growth plans to these confirmed federal policies. The Treasury’s proposed 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit rules are another piece of the equation. Management’s point was that if they can keep lowering the carbon intensity of their fuels by capturing more CO2 and better tracking where their raw materials come from, the value of their carbon credits should go up. So, assuming they execute, that’s a real tailwind.

The impact of the Iran war is real, but not the main driver. Fuel prices have gone up, and that’s helped lead to emergency federal support allowing broader summer E15 sales. In that case, directionally, that’s positive for ethanol demand.

  • Target ethanol capacity expansion: 75M gallons/year.
  • Potential total site capacity (future): 150M gallons/year.
  • Potential future CO₂ capture (expanded site): >400,000 metric tons/year.
  • ATJ-30 potential EBITDA: $150M/year.

Also, U.S. grain prices have gone up since the war started. Yes, that can be good for farmers. But it can also raise costs and increase cash needs across the supply chain. Given that, on one hand, there’s stronger government support and a bigger push for domestic biofuels. On the other hand, you’ve got volatile commodities. That, in turn, leads to shaky logistics and uneven financing, all of which make operations harder to pin down. Overall, the Iran war mostly reinforces what was already in place. And if anything, it strengthens the political case for domestic ethanol.

The main question is whether Gevo can take one successful project and repeat it in other places, without trying to grow too fast or getting ahead of what they can actually execute well. Management clearly sounded more confident that North Dakota can serve as a base for both additional ethanol expansion and their first modular ATJ (alcohol-to-jet) project. And just yesterday, Reuters pointed out that European airlines likely hit or exceeded their SAF mandates, making the demand story look a little less theoretical.

GEVO High Growth Already Reflected

GEVO's priced higher than most traditional energy companies when you look at measures like forward EV-to-sales and EV-to-EBITDA.

The same goes for price-to-sales. It also looks stretched, while book value only helps a little, since it’s just slightly cheaper than similar companies.

At this point, the market is already all-in on the future growth story. Specifically, investors are pricing in big revenue gains expected from the North Dakota integration and the recently announced second expansion there.

Profitability metrics cut against the optimism a bit. The business is still burning cash. Even though EBITDA has improved, the company is still losing money overall (negative EBIT), isn’t generating strong returns on its investments (low returns on equity and capital), and is spending more cash than it brings in after debt costs (negative levered free cash flow).

Those dynamics aren’t exactly risk-free. The expansion plans and the ATJ platform could end up increasing how much cash the company burns, or force it to raise money by issuing more shares. And the overall environment isn’t getting easier. On top of that, the Iran conflict shouldn't be totally dismissed. Things like the margin between input and output prices (feedstock spreads), transportation costs (freight), and the value of credits are all fluctuating more than usual. Plus, competition in low-carbon ethanol and SAF is picking up. As more companies pile in, they end up bidding against each other for the same inputs. All things considered, they need to deliver on their 2026 EBITDA growth and successfully make money from carbon as planned. If they don’t hit those targets fairly cleanly, that makes the current valuation look harder to back up.

Final Takeaway

Hold. GEVO's your classic high-risk, high-reward situation. There’s a lot of upside if their North Dakota ethanol and carbon setup grows successfully the way they expect.

But there’s also a significant downside if GEVO keeps burning cash, has to issue more shares, or struggles to execute in a competitive market.

data.title: A Modest Increase In Italian Inflation But Substantial Risks Remain
data.description:
By Paolo Pizzoli, Senior Economist, Italy, Greece

Inflation accelerates as energy push outweighs leisure and tourism drag

Italian inflation accelerated in March to 1.7% (from 1.5% in February), according to Istat preliminary data, slightly below expectations. Unsurprisingly, at the heart of the acceleration were regulated and non-regulated energy prices together with fresh food, which outweighed the deceleration in leisure, restaurants, and accommodation services.

If the energy push is the inevitable consequence of the war in the Middle East on energy and fuel prices, the slowdown in the leisure and accommodation components likely reflects a normalisation of prices after the boost seen in February with the Milano Cortina Winter Olympics. The latter disinflationary boost, which helped push core inflation back to 1.9% (from 2.4% in February), looks set to disappear in April.

Fuel push already there, electricity will accelerate soon

With the impact of the Middle East war hitting first international oil and gas prices, the shock has been apparent in the transportation component, where petrol and diesel fuel prices are nested, with a combined weight of more than 4% in the total inflation basket. Gas prices edged up 5.9% on the month, and electricity prices gained a meagre 0.5% on the month, a normal delay due to the price formation mechanism, but that will soon accelerate. ARERA, the national energy and grid authority, pre-announced yesterday that energy bills for disadvantaged households will increase by 8% starting from the second quarter.

Pass-through unfolding for manufacturers, but little evidence of wage-price spiral

Looking ahead, it seems inevitable that pass-through will continue, particularly in sectors feeling the impact of the war on energy costs and supply chains. In the March manufacturing business survey, the pricing intention component accelerated markedly, reaching the highest level since March 2023. Pricing intentions in construction remained stable, but much of the acceleration had already occurred in the first two months of 2026, as costs had risen earlier.

For now, the good news comes from the service sector, where pricing intentions have remained stable, suggesting that, for the time being, the risk of a wage-price spiral remains low.

Headline inflation set to break above 2% in April

The acceleration in March inflation data is not surprising, given the ongoing war's impact on fuel and gas prices. It will filter through to energy bills soon, likely pushing headline inflation well above the 2% bar already in April. In non-extreme scenarios, inflation could hover in the 2%-3% band throughout the rest of the year, but the risk of it exceeding 3% is substantial.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
